data.title: Meta, Please Kill Reality Labs
data.description:
Meta (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:META) has underperformed the market since we last recommended against investing in it. The company has hit cumulative losses of more than $60 billion for Reality Labs since late 2020, and now thanks to artificial intelligence, the company's capex is expected to double from 2023 to 2025.

Despite the company's inability to "right-size" its bets, which we'll discuss in more detail below, the incredible strength of its core business and the strong cash flow there make the company a valuable investment.

Meta Revenue

The company managed to grow its revenue signifcantly year-over-year, a substantial improvement given its size.

The company managed to generate more than $42 billion in revenue in the quarter, the vast majority from the US and Canada. However, all of the company's segments and countries outperformed year-over-year, there's no standout region that's underperforming substantially. That revenue improvement is still virtually entirely through the company's ad business.

However, it's key to the company's ability to drive shareholder returns on a $1.5 trillion valuation.

Meta Segment Results

As seen here, the company's Realty Labs continues to generate minimal revenue, with a strong QoQ and YoY decline, and TTM revenue of $2.1 billion, a mere 11% of the division's TTM losses.

Specifically, the company's advertising revenue saw almost 20% growth from $36 billion to more than $41 billion YoY. Outside of the last quarter, which is traditionally the company's strongest, the company had record strong revenue. 1Q 2024 also represented substantial strength over 1Q 2023 as the company showed a trend of improvements.

The company's Realty Labs actually saw revenue decline YoY though as the segment has continued to struggle to gather any meaningful traction. More importantly, losses in the segment expanded YoY to more than $4.2 billion despite the company's goal to manage its expenditures. The division has cost the company $18.1 billion in operating losses over the last 12 months.

That's 20% of the company's operating income, a massive loss on a "bet." The company has maintained a strong operating margin as a result of success in its advertising business along with manageable fixed costs.

Meta Expenses

The company has continued to manage its expenditures while investing heavily in R&D.

The company maintaining fixed or improving margins is key to the company's ability to continue driving shareholder returns. Specifically, despite the company's massive Realty Labs spending, it needs to show a consistent ability to manage its expenditures across the board. The company has reduced its expenses, but it still needs to grow profits more to generate returns.

Meta Capital Expenditures

As a part of this, the company has spent massive amounts of growing cash on capital expenditures.

The company expects 2025 capex to potentially hit $70 billion, a massive jump over 2024, which was already a massive jump over 2023. The company's capex is now well over double where it was in 2023 as the company continues to chase its goals across artificial intelligence. However, the company is continuing to open source its AI, which could hurt future profits.

Take other groundbreaking technologies, such as Windows (closed source) or Linux (open source), and the revenue differences. This isn't to say that there's no opportunity to make money off of open-source technology, but when you take a stance to not charge for the technology you're spending tens of billions on, you owe investors a clear path to justify that investment.

Meta has not presented one.

Meta Growth

However, Meta is continuing to see consistent growth across its portfolio of assets.

The company's daily active users grew from 3.24 billion to 3.43 billion. That's high single-digit growth as the company has continued to see substantial popularity across what's an impressive portfolio of assets. It's also substantial because the company now has almost half of the population globally as daily users on its apps.

That shows the scale of the company's operations as it continues to grow. But it also shows the company's limitations. It won't be able to triple in size from daily active users because there's not enough people on the planet.

The company has done a fantastic job of earning more from the same customers. The company has managed to deliver mid-single-digit growth in ad impressions to the company's customers. Continuing to grow this number along with substantial growth from Q1'23 to Q1'24 is key to the company continuing its revenue growth.

At the same time, the company has managed to increase its price per impression by double digits from a worldwide perspective. This is with key performance in some markets like the US and Canada. That growth is essential along with the company's impressions to growing overall revenue, and the company has managed to tick it up in recent quarters.

That shows substantial demand in the market for the company's offerings and it's key to the company driving overall shareholder returns.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is Meta's lack of ability to "right-size" its bets. Take Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) for example. Google's "Other Bets" incorporates bets like Waymo, Wing, Google Fiber, etc., plenty of unique and interesting ideas. The "Other Bets" division at Google earns ~$450 million in quarterly revenue, similar to what Realty Labs is earning.

However, operating income losses from the division are just more than $1 billion, or 25% of what Meta is losing on Reality Labs alone. A new idea or investment isn't just dependent on how much it earns, it's dependent on how much the company needs to invest and the return on invested capital. Meta's over-investment (i.e. non right-sized bets) in ideas that haven't proven their ability to generate sufficient returns are a substantial risk to long-term shareholder returns.

Conclusion

Meta is a company we've soured on before as the company continues to spend tens of billions on new initiatives with questionable return potential. Whether that's Reality Labs or artificial intelligence, the company has surpassed $60 billion in Reality Labs spending and experts question whether the company will ever earn a return.

The company, however, does have an incredibly strong core business. The company is growing customers, ad impressions per customers, and ad profits per impression. That's a triple whammy that's leading to growing cash flow for the company, making it a valuable long-term investment.

data.title: Frontera Energy: Guyana Cancels The Petroleum Agreement And The Prospecting License
data.description:
Frontera Energy Corporation (OTCPK:FECCF) offered the following explanation to shareholders as part of its annual reporting:

Given the reporting referenced in the last article noting the Guyana government's stance, the conclusion of the situation has to be regarded as optimistic.

In March 2025, the company received a communication from the government of Guyana notifying the company that basically all rights to the lease are being cancelled.

"The Government is of the view that the Petroleum Agreement and Prospecting Licence are at an end," but on the other hand, the Government of Guyana "hereby terminates the Petroleum Agreement and cancels the Prospecting Licence with immediate effect."

The above quote noted in the March press release should be no surprise given the events noted in the previous article. Management was given time to make representations to the government as to why that quote should not be issued. Evidently, the government saw no convincing reputation.

But this calls into question the asset values on the balance sheet as this dispute has basically headed in one direction with really no legal action by the company since the process began by the Guyana government to terminate the lease.

Now, in Canada, the accounting system is a bit different in that impairments are routinely recovered if that happens to be the adjustment needed. Therefore, one would have to believe that these assets have less value going forward (combined with the belief of course that management intends to defend its claim and all that goes with it). There have been some public statements by management both during reporting and also in other public venues that the current situation apparently impairs the company's ability to move forward with the leases. But that would be expected given the government's position.

The reason is that, realistically, those assets are now out of the control of the company. It's an allowed treatment that the company can have the position of the full value of the assets. Many companies do not take that route in a dispute like this.

This type of dispute between management and Guyana can be challenging for investors. Understand that this dispute shows a big variation from industry practice as shown by many other companies that I follow. An investor does not have to publicly take sides to conclude that the risk of asset overvaluation is very high in a case like this. Hence, an impairment charge in the near future would not be a surprise at all.

Now, the remaining businesses of the company appear to be just fine. The company will be reporting its results in a couple of days. Should it take an impairment charge, that impairment charge will be non-cash. It's far more likely that weakening oil prices, trade wars and tariff threats dominate the company outlook.

With the annual report, management did mention that the debt ratings were largely reaffirmed. That's probably a step in the right direction.

But left out of the discussion is management's reputation for conservative presentations of the company position. To its credit, management has presented both sides of the dispute with Guyana. What shareholders are waiting for is a legal strategy that essentially verifies the management position. So far, that has not happened. That affects management credibility.

Upcoming Report

The remaining operations are largely centered in South America. Again, there's a strategic alternative process underway for at least some of the Colombian assets that has yet to have any tangible results. Given what has happened in Guyana, this management is in desperate need of tangible results that meet at least someone's expectations.

Stock Price

Since I began the more recent series of articles on this situation, the stock price has not done well.

At the center of the stock price downward action has to be the Guyana dispute. The government has long held that Frontera has not met its lease obligations. There's also a secondary government position that the company is too small to finance the lease obligations so that the current dispute (if allowed to continue) would be a recurring problem for the foreseeable future.

Frontera has been repeatedly asked for financial assurances that the current situation could be remedied by appropriate financial resources. Management has apparently failed to satisfy the government that it can meet its obligations by showing it has adequate financial resources. Hence, the government decision is shown above.

Africa Oil Example

Africa Oil Corp. (OTCPK:AOIFF) is another small player in the giant offshore industry. The challenge that many of these small players have, similar to Frontera, is giant financial obligations compared to the size of the company. Africa Oil sold much of its interests in several of these projects in return for a cash carry. The company it sold to was TotalEnergies SE (TTE). Total has absolutely no problems convincing anyone it can meet its obligations while Africa Oil still retains a significant profit interest (for the size of the company).

For many of these small companies, this comes under risk reduction. For the country that manages the lease obligations, the finances are no longer an issue.

For some reason, Frontera went with a strategy that managed to bypass this option (at least so far).

Summary

Until this management establishes a strong positive track record, this company remains a strong sell. Frontera is now in a situation that most managements would tell you they go to great lengths to avoid. The risk of loss of all the money spent in Guyana (and possibly Suriname later) is probably significant.

Anytime an investor sees a company going against industry practice (as is the case here) it's extremely wise to stay on the sidelines until a solid record of accomplishment is established. Management could still succeed in the situation with Guyana. But there's no reason for an investor to risk money until that success becomes apparent. Too many times, the long-term price action shown above is the result.

Risks

The biggest risk by far is the management strategy that has generally gone against offshore industry practice. Such strategies seldom succeed. The few that do succeed are often not worth the chances taken by investors.

Here there's a chance of some big legal bills ahead combined with a speculative chance that it's worth the effort.

The whole thing calls into question the strategic alternatives process that is continuing in Colombia. There, management needs a solid result to help repair what is going on in Guyana. Otherwise, this management will have little standing in the eyes of the market.

That makes the coming quarterly reports (and the ones that follow) very important for interested investors to follow. For me, there is far less risk elsewhere.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Potbelly: A Strong Start To 2025
data.description:
Potbelly (NASDAQ:PBPB) delivered +0.9% company-operated same-store sales growth in Q1 2025 despite an estimated 1.5% negative impact from severe winter weather. This was a strong result that exceeded its expectations by a couple percent and positions it well to meet or exceed its full-year guidance. This is despite a challenging economic environment that has resulted in some other restaurant chains trimming their sales expectations by a couple percent.

Potbelly's adjusted EBITDA results were also strong during the quarter as it exceeded expectations by around $1 million.

Despite the strong Q1 2025 results, Potbelly is maintaining its full-year guidance instead of increasing it. This appears wise given the increasing uncertainty around the economic environment.

I have increased my estimate of Potbelly's value by $0.50 to a new range of $10 to $11 per share. I am neutral on Potbelly at its current price. I believe Potbelly will gain market share overall, but am not increasing its estimated value by more due to the uncertain economic environment.

Q1 2025 Results

Potbelly exceeded expectations in Q1 2025. It reported +0.9% company-operated same-store sales growth, which was a couple percent better than the midpoint of its guidance for -1.5% to -0.5% same-store sales growth. This was driven by a 1.1% increase in the average check, while transactions were down by 0.2%.

The solid sales performance contributed to Potbelly's adjusted EBITDA reaching $5.5 million, higher than its guidance range of $3.5 million to $4.5 million in adjusted EBITDA for the quarter.

These Q1 2025 results were achieved despite some significant winter weather impacts in the Midwest and Texas that reduced Potbelly's sales growth by around 1.5% and its adjusted EBITDA by around $0.6 million for the quarter. Without that winter weather impact, it appears that Potbelly's same-store transaction growth would have been slightly positive. The average check increase was a couple percent lower than Potbelly's average gross menu price increase, suggesting a slight shift towards value hunting.

Potbelly reported $5.7 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q1 2024, but that was with the benefit of a $1.1 million settlement payment. The settlement payment was from a third-party software vendor that had a service disruption that previously impacted Potbelly's shop operations.

Adjusting for that, Potbelly's adjusted EBITDA increased by $0.9 million year-over-year, helped by a 31% increase in franchise-related income and a reduction in food, beverage and packaging costs from 27.2% of net sandwich shop sales to 26.1%.

Potbelly had mentioned expectations for 2025 food (and overall) inflation that was close to the projected increases in its gross menu price. That would keep its food, beverage and packaging costs relatively constant as a percentage of net sandwich shop sales. In Q1 2025 it reported a slight deflation in food costs instead, but it is still being cautious in expecting food inflation later on in the year.

Notes On 2025 Guidance

Potbelly has maintained its full-year guidance for +1.5% to +2.5% same-store sales growth along with adjusted EBITDA of $33 million to $34 million.

Given its outperformance in Q1 2025 (and in late 2024), it seems to be on a path to meet or exceed that guidance. The overall economic environment is somewhat uncertain though, so Potbelly will probably continue to be conservative with changes to its full-year guidance.

Various fast-casual and fast food restaurant chains such as Chipotle and Wendy's have noted the challenging environment and tweaked their same-store sales growth expectations downward by a couple percentage points.

In the context of the economic environment, Potbelly is performing pretty well so far and gaining share.

Franchise Contributions

Potbelly's franchises are continuing to increase in importance. Potbelly's franchise margins (defined as franchise royalties, fees and rent income less franchise support, rent and marketing expenses) ended up at approximately $4.9 million in 2024, up +45% compared to $3.4 million in 2023.

For Q1 2025, Potbelly's franchise margins ended up around $1.6 million, a roughly 52% increase from the approximately $1.0 million in Q1 2024.

This trend is expected to continue as Potbelly adds new franchise shops as well as re-franchising company-operated locations.

Notes On Estimated Value

I previously estimated Potbelly's value at $9.50 to $10.50 per share. Potbelly's continued solid performance and its incremental improvements in adjusted EBITDA boost my estimate of its value to $10 to $11 per share.

Potbelly expects a roughly $1 million year-over-year increase in full-year adjusted EBITDA, which would be approximately $2 million if the settlement payment from the third-party software vendor is excluded.

I am assuming Potbelly meets its full-year guidance and am taking a somewhat conservative outlook due to the economic uncertainty.

Potbelly repurchased around 117,000 shares in Q1 2025 for $1.1 million. This rate of repurchase is a bit less than what is generally needed to offset stock-based compensation. Potbelly granted approximately 411,000 RSUs and 212,000 PSUs in 2024.

Conclusion

Potbelly's Q1 2025 performance was positive, with its same-store sales growth ending up a couple percent above expectations, while its adjusted EBITDA was around $1 million above the midpoint of its guidance range.

There are some concerns around the economic environment, so Potbelly has maintained its full-year guidance despite the strong start to the year. I'd expect Potbelly to at least meet that guidance unless the economic environment deteriorates significantly.

I now estimate Potbelly's value at $10 to $11 per share, a $0.50 per share increase from my last estimate. I thus remain neutral on it at its current share price.
